cross_references.title: Global Health Systems and Universal Health Coverage
cross_references.relevance: Foundation concepts for health system functions and UHC frameworks

cross_references.title: Global Health Policy and Governance
cross_references.relevance: Policy frameworks and international health regulations

cross_references.title: Health Equity and Human Rights
cross_references.relevance: Equity measurement and right to health framework

cross_references.title: Global Health Partnerships and Initiatives
cross_references.relevance: External financing through global health initiatives

cross_references.title: Global Health Research and Evidence
cross_references.relevance: Research methods for economic evaluation and implementation science

learning_objectives.text: Define fundamental health economics concepts including scarcity, opportunity cost, efficiency, and equity and explain their relevance to health resource allocation decisions
Compare and contrast different health financing mechanisms including taxation, social health insurance, community-based insurance, and out-of-pocket payments across diverse country contexts
Apply cost-effectiveness analysis methods to evaluate health interventions using appropriate metrics including cost per DALY averted and incremental cost-effectiveness ratios
Analyze health financing systems for their impact on financial protection, including measurement of catastrophic health expenditure and impoverishment
Evaluate health care market failures and regulatory approaches in different country contexts including public-private partnerships
Design appropriate health financing reform strategies considering country-specific contexts, fiscal space, and equity objectives

key_terms.definition: The value of the next best alternative foregone when making a resource allocation decision
The distribution of resources among different uses to maximize health outcomes or social welfare
Producing maximum output from a given set of inputs, or producing a given output with minimum inputs
An economic evaluation method comparing the costs and health outcomes of alternative interventions
The ratio of the difference in costs to the difference in health outcomes between two interventions
A monetary value below which an intervention is considered cost-effective in a given context
A health financing mechanism where contributions are mandatory and usually linked to employment
Spreading financial risk across a population so that no individual faces the full cost of health care alone
Out-of-pocket health spending exceeding a defined threshold of household income or consumption
Ensuring that health care costs do not cause financial hardship or impoverishment
The tendency for insured individuals to use more health services than they would if paying out-of-pocket
The tendency for higher-risk individuals to be more likely to purchase health insurance
Public spending on health from domestic government sources
Health spending from external sources including development assistance for health
The capacity of government to provide additional budgetary resources for health without jeopardizing fiscal sustainability
A financing system where contributions increase as a proportion of income for wealthier individuals
A financing system where contributions represent a higher proportion of income for poorer individuals
Active decisions about which services to purchase, from whom, and how to pay for them
Payment mechanisms that link financing to the achievement of predetermined health results
Systematic evaluation of health technologies to inform coverage and reimbursement decisions

case_studies.title: Thailand's Universal Coverage Scheme: Achieving Financial Protection through Health Financing Reform
case_studies.focus: Health financing reform for universal coverage
key_lessons: Political commitment and sustained investment enable rapid coverage expansion
Tax-based financing can achieve broad population coverage in middle-income countries
Closed-end budgets with capitation payments can control costs while maintaining quality
Strong purchasing arrangements and provider payment reform are essential for sustainability

case_studies.title: Cost-Effectiveness of Rotavirus Vaccine Introduction in Bangladesh
key_lessons: Economic evaluation informs evidence-based immunization policy decisions
Context-specific cost-effectiveness analysis is essential for local decision-making
Disability-adjusted life years provide a comprehensive measure of health impact
Vaccine introduction decisions require consideration of both health and economic outcomes

case_studies.title: India's Ayushman Bharat PM-JAY: Large-Scale Financial Protection for the Poor
key_lessons: Large-scale government insurance programs can rapidly expand financial protection coverage
Technology platforms enable efficient enrollment and claims processing at scale
Benefit package design affects both financial protection and program sustainability
Monitoring catastrophic expenditure outcomes is essential for program evaluation

case_studies.title: Results-Based Financing in Rwanda: Linking Payments to Health Outcomes
key_lessons: Performance-based incentives can improve health service delivery quality and coverage
Verification systems are essential for results-based financing credibility
Community health insurance complements facility-based performance incentives
Sustained political commitment and donor support enable program scaling

sdg_alignment.description: Achieve universal health coverage including financial risk protection
Implement social protection systems and measures for all
Adopt policies especially fiscal policies that progressively achieve greater equality
